metadata.title: Oncolytic Virotherapy — T-VEC, the FDA-Approved Herpes Virus That Fights Cancer

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Nik Bear Brown. T-VEC — the FDA-approved herpes virus that fights cancer.

T-VEC is a herpes simplex virus engineered to infect and lyse cancer cells, and to secrete GM-CSF to recruit the immune system. It is FDA-approved for melanoma. Injected directly into a tumor, it works. But the same strategy delivered systemically — through the bloodstream — has failed repeatedly. What does direct injection accomplish that systemic delivery cannot?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: T-VEC is a herpes simplex virus engineered to infect and lyse cancer cells, and to secrete GM-CSF to recruit the immune system. It is FDA-approved for melanoma.

beats.narration_text: Research oncolytic virotherapy: T-VEC mechanism, OPTiM trial results, why systemic delivery fails, engineering modifications being tested, and the current clinical pipeline beyond melanoma.

props.command: claude "Research oncolytic virotherapy: T-VEC mechanism (modified HSV-1, deleted ICP34.5, added GM-CSF), the OPTiM trial results (durable response rate vs GM-CSF), why systemic delivery fails (neutralizing antibodies, liver sequestration, innate immune clearance), what engineering modifications are being tested to overcome systemic barriers, and the current clinical pipeline beyond melanoma."

beats.narration_text: Python script comparing intratumoral versus systemic delivery outcomes.

props.code:
STRATEGIES = [
    {"delivery": "Intratumoral (T-VEC)", "virus": "HSV-1 modified",
     "DRR_pct": 16.3, "barrier": "Requires accessible lesion", "status": "FDA APPROVED"},
    {"delivery": "Systemic IV (Pexa-Vec)", "virus": "Vaccinia",
     "DRR_pct": 0.0,  "barrier": "Neutralizing Ab + liver sequestration", "status": "Phase 3 FAILED"},
    {"delivery": "Systemic (ONCOS-102)",  "virus": "Adenovirus",
     "DRR_pct": None, "barrier": "Innate immune clearance", "status": "Phase 1/2"},
    {"delivery": "Cell carrier (OV-CAR-3)","virus": "Measles",
     "DRR_pct": None, "barrier": "Cell survival after infusion", "status": "Preclinical"},
]
for s in STRATEGIES:
    drr = f"{s['DRR_pct']:.1f}%" if s["DRR_pct"] is not None else "not reported"
    print(f"[{s['status']:<18}] {s['delivery']:<30}  DRR: {drr}")
    print(f"   Barrier: {s['barrier']}")

beats.narration_text: T-VEC works because it converts a cold tumor — immunologically invisible — into a hot one by forcing antigen release and immune recruitment in situ. That local effect is detectable even in distant uninjected lesions. The challenge for systemic delivery is getting the virus past neutralizing antibodies without losing the ability to replicate in tumor cells. The engineering solutions — polymer coating, cell carrier vehicles, stealth modifications — are all in early trials.

beats.narration_text: Read Andtbacka et al. 2015 in JCO — the OPTiM trial. Find the abscopal response rate: tumors not injected with T-VEC that nonetheless responded. That number is the evidence the immune mechanism is real. Then check whether the abscopal responders had higher CD8+ T cell infiltration at baseline.
